title: Fixing Invalid Git Commit Hash Validation

statement.markdown: Write a function to validate a Git commit hash. A valid Git commit hash is a 40 character hexadecimal string. The function should return `true` for valid hashes and `false` otherwise. Note that the input may contain non-hexadecimal characters or be shorter/longer than 40 characters. You should only check for hexadecimal and length validity.
statement.inputFormat: A single string containing the commit hash to be validated
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the commit hash is valid
statement.notes: Be mindful of case sensitivity and whitespace handling.

examples.explanation: Hexadecimal string but not 40 characters long

hints.text: Use regular expressions to check for hexadecimal characters only

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: A simple approach would be to check the length and then verify that all characters are hexadecimal.
editorial.bruteForce: One could create a function to check each character individually, but this would be inefficient and error-prone.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a regular expression to check for hexadecimal characters in one step.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The regular expression ensures that each character is hexadecimal, and the length check ensures the correct length.
commonMistakes: Only checking length without verifying hexadecimal characters

starterCode.python:
class Solution:
    def is_valid_commit_hash(self, hash: str) -> bool: /* implementation */
